name: Three Dimensional Geometry
subject: Maths

sections.heading: 11.1 Direction Cosines & Direction Ratios of a Line
sections.body:
A line in space makes angles $\alpha,\beta,\gamma$ with the positive $x,y,z$ axes. Its direction cosines are $l=\cos\alpha,\,m=\cos\beta,\,n=\cos\gamma$ with $l^{2}+m^{2}+n^{2}=1.$

Direction ratios $a,b,c$: any triple proportional to $l,m,n$. Given d.r. $a,b,c$:

$l=\dfrac{a}{\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}}},\;m=\dfrac{b}{\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}}},\;n=\dfrac{c}{\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}}}.$

For the line through $A(x_1,y_1,z_1)$ and $B(x_2,y_2,z_2)$, d.r. are $x_2-x_1,\,y_2-y_1,\,z_2-z_1$.

sections.heading: 11.2 Equation of a Line
sections.body:
Vector form through point $\vec a$ with direction $\vec b$: $\vec r=\vec a+\lambda\vec b,\;\lambda\in\mathbb R.$

Cartesian form through $(x_1,y_1,z_1)$ with d.r. $a,b,c$:

$\dfrac{x-x_1}{a}=\dfrac{y-y_1}{b}=\dfrac{z-z_1}{c}.$

Through two points $(x_1,y_1,z_1)$ and $(x_2,y_2,z_2)$:

$\dfrac{x-x_1}{x_2-x_1}=\dfrac{y-y_1}{y_2-y_1}=\dfrac{z-z_1}{z_2-z_1}.$

Vector form: $\vec r=\vec a+\lambda(\vec b-\vec a)$.

sections.heading: 11.3 Angle Between Two Lines
sections.body:
If lines have direction vectors $\vec b_1$ and $\vec b_2$ (or d.r. $(a_1,b_1,c_1)$ and $(a_2,b_2,c_2)$), the acute angle $\theta$ between them satisfies

$\cos\theta=\dfrac{|\vec b_1\cdot\vec b_2|}{|\vec b_1||\vec b_2|}=\dfrac{|a_1 a_2+b_1 b_2+c_1 c_2|}{\sqrt{a_1^{2}+b_1^{2}+c_1^{2}}\sqrt{a_2^{2}+b_2^{2}+c_2^{2}}}.$

Two lines are perpendicular iff $a_1 a_2+b_1 b_2+c_1 c_2=0$ and parallel iff $\dfrac{a_1}{a_2}=\dfrac{b_1}{b_2}=\dfrac{c_1}{c_2}.$

sections.heading: 11.4 Shortest Distance Between Two Lines
sections.body:
Two lines that are not coplanar are skew; their shortest distance is the length of the common perpendicular.

Skew lines. $\vec r=\vec a_1+\lambda\vec b_1$ and $\vec r=\vec a_2+\mu\vec b_2$:

$d=\dfrac{\big|\,(\vec a_2-\vec a_1)\cdot(\vec b_1\times\vec b_2)\,\big|}{|\vec b_1\times\vec b_2|}.$

Parallel lines. If $\vec b_1\parallel\vec b_2$: $d=\dfrac{|(\vec a_2-\vec a_1)\times\vec b_1|}{|\vec b_1|}.$

Two lines are coplanar iff the shortest distance is zero, i.e. $(\vec a_2-\vec a_1)\cdot(\vec b_1\times\vec b_2)=0$.
keyFormulae: $d=\dfrac{|(\vec a_2-\vec a_1)\cdot(\vec b_1\times\vec b_2)|}{|\vec b_1\times\vec b_2|}$

sections.heading: 11.5 Equation of a Plane
sections.body:
Vector form (normal $\hat n$, distance $d$ from origin): $\vec r\cdot\hat n=d$ (normal form, $d\geq 0$).

Cartesian (normal form): $lx+my+nz=d$ where $l,m,n$ are direction cosines of the unit normal.

Through a point with given normal $\vec n$: $(\vec r-\vec a)\cdot\vec n=0$, i.e. $a(x-x_1)+b(y-y_1)+c(z-z_1)=0.$

Through three points $A,B,C$: $\vec n=(\vec{AB})\times(\vec{AC})$ then use $(\vec r-\vec a)\cdot\vec n=0$.

Intercept form: $\dfrac{x}{a}+\dfrac{y}{b}+\dfrac{z}{c}=1.$

sections.heading: 11.6 Plane Through Intersection of Two Planes
sections.body:
Given two planes $\vec r\cdot\vec n_1=d_1$ and $\vec r\cdot\vec n_2=d_2$, any plane through their intersection has the form

$\vec r\cdot(\vec n_1+\lambda\vec n_2)=d_1+\lambda d_2,\quad \lambda\in\mathbb R.$

Cartesian: $(a_1 x+b_1 y+c_1 z-d_1)+\lambda(a_2 x+b_2 y+c_2 z-d_2)=0$.

sections.heading: 11.7 Angles & Distances Involving Planes
sections.body:
Angle between two planes (acute): $\cos\theta=\dfrac{|\vec n_1\cdot\vec n_2|}{|\vec n_1||\vec n_2|}.$ Planes are perpendicular iff $\vec n_1\cdot\vec n_2=0$, parallel iff $\vec n_1\parallel\vec n_2$.

Angle between line and plane: if $\vec b$ is the line's direction and $\vec n$ the plane's normal, then $\sin\phi=\dfrac{|\vec b\cdot\vec n|}{|\vec b||\vec n|}.$

Distance from point to plane. For point $(x_1,y_1,z_1)$ and plane $ax+by+cz+d=0$: $D=\dfrac{|ax_1+by_1+cz_1+d|}{\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}}}.$

summary: Lines in 3D are described by a point and a direction (vector or Cartesian symmetric form). Angles between lines come from the dot product of direction vectors. Skew lines have shortest distance $\dfrac{|(\vec a_2-\vec a_1)\cdot(\vec b_1\times\vec b_2)|}{|\vec b_1\times\vec b_2|}$. Planes are described by a point and a normal $\vec n$, giving $(\vec r-\vec a)\cdot\vec n=0$ or $ax+by+cz+d=0$. Intercept form makes $x,y,z$-axis crossings explicit. Angle between line and plane uses $\sin$ of the angle between $\vec b$ and $\vec n$. Distance from point to plane is the standard normal-projection formula.
pitfalls: Using $\cos$ instead of $\sin$ for the angle between a line and a plane.
Forgetting absolute value when computing acute angles.
Confusing direction ratios with direction cosines (the latter are normalized).
Treating skew lines as intersecting just because the shortest distance is small.
Wrong sign of $d$ in $\vec r\cdot\hat n=d$ — keep $d\geq 0$ in normal form.
